name: Credit risk
description: Loss from borrowers and issuers failing to pay: parameters, models, products.

glossary.PD: Probability of Default — the likelihood a borrower defaults over a stated horizon
glossary.LGD: Loss Given Default — the fraction of exposure lost if default occurs, after recoveries
glossary.EAD: Exposure at Default — the expected amount owed at the moment of default
glossary.EL: Expected Loss — the product of PD, LGD and EAD, representing the average credit loss
glossary.CCF: Credit Conversion Factor — the fraction of an undrawn commitment assumed to be drawn by the time of default
glossary.CDS: Credit Default Swap — a contract paying out on a reference entity's default event in exchange for periodic premium payments
glossary.ECL: Expected Credit Loss — the forward-looking accounting provisioning measure under IFRS 9
glossary.NPL: Non-Performing Loan — a loan that is 90+ days past due or unlikely to be repaid in full
glossary.IRB: Internal Ratings-Based approach — a Basel framework allowing approved banks to use their own credit risk parameter estimates
glossary.CDO: Collateralised Debt Obligation — a securitisation of a pool of debt (bonds, loans) sold as rated tranches
glossary.CRM: Credit Risk Mitigation — techniques reducing credit exposure (collateral, guarantees, netting) recognised in capital rules
glossary.TTC: Through-the-Cycle — a PD estimation approach averaging over the full economic cycle for stability
glossary.PIT: Point-in-Time — a PD estimation approach reflecting current economic conditions
glossary.ASRF: Asymptotic Single-Risk-Factor model — the single-factor, infinite-granularity credit portfolio model underlying the Basel IRB capital formula

primer:
**Credit risk** is loss when a borrower/counterparty fails to pay — the largest risk for most banks. It decomposes into **expected loss** $\\mathrm{EL}=\\mathrm{PD}\\times\\mathrm{LGD}\\times\\mathrm{EAD}$.

**Default** (Basel, for capital): **90+** days past due *or* 'unlikely to pay'. The precise definition drives every PD model — a softer one lowers measured PD. **PD** = probability of default over a horizon (usually 1yr). **LGD** = fraction lost if default occurs $=1-\\text{recovery rate}$; downturn (stressed) LGD is used for capital. **Recovery rate** depends on seniority and cycle (senior secured >> subordinated unsecured). **EAD** = amount owed at default; for a revolving facility $\\mathrm{EAD}=\\text{drawn}+\\mathrm{CCF}\\times\\text{undrawn}$, where the **CCF** (credit conversion factor) is the fraction of the undrawn commitment assumed drawn (distressed borrowers draw down, so CCF is well above zero). An **obligor rating** captures PD; a **facility rating** captures LGD — so two facilities to one obligor share a PD but can differ in LGD.

**Ratings** (AAA…D) map to PD. The *investment-grade vs high-yield* boundary is **BBB−**. Ratings are *through-the-cycle* (stable, lag market signals). **TTC** PD averages over the cycle (stable capital); **PIT** PD reflects current conditions (pricing, IFRS 9). A **migration/transition matrix** $P_{ij}=\\Pr(j\\text{ at }t{+}1\\mid i\\text{ at }t)$ drives credit VaR and mark-to-market rating drift.

**Market signals.** *Credit spread* $\\approx\\mathrm{PD}\\times\\mathrm{LGD}+$ risk & liquidity premium. A *CDS* pays out on a credit event for a premium; its spread is a clean read on PD×LGD. The *CDS–bond basis* = CDS spread − bond Z-spread (funding, repo, cheapest-to-deliver).

**Models.** *Hazard rate* $\\lambda$ = instantaneous default rate given survival; $\\Pr(\\tau>t)=e^{-\\int_0^t\\lambda\\,ds}$, constant $\\lambda\\Rightarrow\\mathrm{PD}(t)=1-e^{-\\lambda t}$. *Merton structural*: equity is a call on assets struck at debt; default if $V_T<D$; **distance to default** $\\mathrm{DD}=\\frac{\\ln(V/D)+(\\mu-\\frac12\\sigma^2)T}{\\sigma\\sqrt T}$ converts to PD via the **normal CDF**, $\\mathrm{PD}=N(-\\mathrm{DD})$. *Reduced-form*: default is a jump at a market-calibrated hazard rate (no balance sheet) — standard for pricing. *Credit VaR* is the loss quantile (e.g. 99.9%, 1yr); the distribution is skewed and fat-tailed (CreditMetrics/CreditRisk+/KMV).

**Portfolio.** *Default correlation* (shared exposure to the economy) is the dominant, hardest-to-estimate driver of tail risk — underestimating it drove 2008 losses. *Gaussian copula*: $X_i=\\sqrt{\\rho}M+\\sqrt{1-\\rho}\\varepsilon_i$, default if $X_i<N^{-1}(\\mathrm{PD}_i)$ (thin tails understate joint defaults). *Vasicek ASRF* (one factor, infinite granularity) gives a portfolio-invariant closed-form tail loss — the basis of the Basel IRB formula. *Concentration/single-name risk* is assumed away by ASRF, so Pillar 2 adds it back.

**Accounting & products.** *IFRS 9 ECL*: forward-looking, probability-weighted across scenarios, $\\mathrm{ECL}=\\sum_s p_s(\\mathrm{PD}_s\\mathrm{LGD}_s\\mathrm{EAD}_s)$; *staging* — Stage 1 (12-month ECL), Stage 2 (SICR → lifetime ECL), Stage 3 (impaired). An **NPL** is **90+** days past due or unlikely to be repaid; NPL ratio = NPLs/total loans. *Provisions* absorb **expected** loss; **capital** absorbs unexpected loss. *Securitisation* pools loans into tradable securities; *tranching/subordination* slices losses into a waterfall (junior/equity first, senior last) — credit enhancement makes senior tranches highly rated. A *CDO* securitises debt into rated tranches, exquisitely sensitive to default correlation. *Credit enhancement*: subordination, overcollateralisation, excess spread, guarantees. *Sovereign risk*: government default/restructuring (sovereign-bank doom loop). *Settlement (Herstatt) risk*: one leg delivers, the other fails — **PvP** systems (CLS) neutralise it by simultaneous legs. *Credit risk mitigation (CRM)*: collateral, guarantees, derivatives, netting — lowers LGD or EAD, and thus capital.

cards.definition: The risk of loss when a borrower or counterparty fails to meet its obligations in full or on time. It is the largest risk for most commercial banks and decomposes into default probability, loss severity and exposure size.

cards.term: Under the Basel default definition, an exposure is treated as in default once it is {{90}} days past due (or 'unlikely to pay').
cards.definition: The credit event of failing to pay, breaching covenants, or entering bankruptcy or restructuring. Basel defines it for capital purposes as 90+ days past due or 'unlikely to pay', which standardises [[PD]] estimation.
cards.note: The precise definition drives every [[PD]] model — a softer definition lowers measured [[PD]].

cards.definition: The likelihood that an obligor defaults over a stated horizon, usually one year. It is estimated from ratings, financial ratios or market signals and is the first input to expected loss, alongside [[LGD]] and [[EAD]], to give [[EL]].

cards.term: Loss given default equals one minus the {{recovery rate}}.
cards.definition: The fraction of exposure actually lost if default occurs, after recoveries from collateral, guarantees and workout — the complement of the recovery rate. Downturn LGD (stressed) is used for capital because recoveries fall in recessions. Works together with [[PD]] and [[EAD]] to determine expected loss.

cards.term: For a revolving facility, EAD is estimated as the drawn balance plus the {{CCF}} times the undrawn commitment.
cards.definition: The expected amount owed at the moment of default, including likely drawdowns on committed but unused lines. For loans it is the drawn balance plus a fraction of the undrawn commitment. Used alongside [[PD]] and [[LGD]] to calculate expected loss.
The proportion of a defaulted exposure ultimately recovered through the workout or asset sale. It depends on seniority, collateral and the economic cycle, and is the empirical input behind [[LGD]].
cards.note: Senior secured recovers far more than subordinated unsecured — seniority is decisive.

cards.definition: The fraction of an undrawn commitment assumed to be drawn by the time of default, converting off-balance-sheet exposure into a loan-equivalent. Borrowers in distress tend to draw down lines, so CCFs are well above zero.
cards.note: A key, and easily understated, driver of [[EAD]] for revolving facilities.

cards.term: An obligor rating captures [[PD]] while a facility rating captures [[LGD]], so two facilities to the same obligor share a [[PD]] but can differ in [[LGD]].
cards.definition: True. An obligor (borrower) rating captures [[PD]] — the chance the entity defaults; a facility rating captures [[LGD]] — how much is lost on a specific loan given its collateral and seniority. Two facilities to the same obligor share a [[PD]] but differ in [[LGD]].
cards.summary: True — the obligor rating reflects default probability; the facility rating reflects loss severity given the collateral and seniority of that specific loan.

cards.term: The investment-grade versus high-yield boundary sits at a rating of {{BBB-}}.
cards.definition: An ordinal assessment of creditworthiness (e.g. AAA to D) mapped to a [[PD]], produced by agencies or internal models. Investment grade (≥BBB−) versus high yield is a critical threshold for mandates and capital. Market signals such as [[CDS]] spreads are more timely but volatile.
cards.note: Ratings are 'through-the-cycle': deliberately stable, so they lag fast-moving market signals.

cards.definition: [[TTC]] [[PD]] averages over the economic cycle for stability (used to avoid procyclical capital in [[IRB]] models); [[PIT]] [[PD]] reflects current conditions and moves with the cycle (used for pricing and IFRS 9). The choice shapes how volatile capital and provisions become.
cards.note: Mixing them up causes either understated risk in booms or violent capital swings.

cards.definition: A table of probabilities that an obligor moves from one rating to another (including default) over a period. It drives credit VaR models and the mark-to-market value change from rating drift, not just outright default. Market-implied [[PD]] and [[CDS]] spreads complement the matrix for real-time signals.
cards.formula: $P_{ij} = \Pr(\text{rating } j \text{ at } t{+}1 \mid \text{rating } i \text{ at } t)$

cards.definition: The yield premium of a risky bond over the risk-free rate, compensating for expected loss plus a risk premium and liquidity. Its widening is the market's real-time signal of deteriorating credit. A [[CDS]] spread offers a cleaner read on [[PD]]×[[LGD]] without cash-bond funding effects.

A contract that pays out on a reference entity's credit event in exchange for periodic premium payments — insurance against default, or a way to take credit risk synthetically. The CDS spread is a clean market read on [[PD]]×[[LGD]].
cards.formula: premium leg $=$ protection leg at inception (fair spread)

cards.definition: The difference between a name's [[CDS]] spread and the credit spread of its cash bond. Persistent non-zero basis reflects funding costs, repo specialness and the cheapest-to-deliver option, and is a classic relative-value (and crisis-stress) signal.

The instantaneous conditional rate of default given survival so far — the parameter of reduced-form models. A constant hazard gives an exponential survival curve, and the cumulative [[PD]] over a horizon follows directly.

Models a firm's equity as a call option on its assets struck at the face value of its debt: default happens if asset value falls below debt at maturity. It links credit risk to equity volatility and leverage, the basis of KMV/Moody's distance-to-default. Outputs a structural [[PD]] and implies a market-consistent [[LGD]].

cards.term: In the Merton framework, a firm's distance to default is converted into a [[PD]] by applying the {{normal CDF}} to the negative distance to default.
cards.definition: The number of asset-value standard deviations between a firm's current asset value and its default point. It converts the Merton model into a [[PD]] via the normal CDF and is intuitive for ranking credit quality. [[IRB]] models use analogous distance metrics.

Models default as an unpredictable jump governed by a hazard rate calibrated to market prices ([[CDS]], bonds), without modelling the firm's balance sheet. It fits market spreads well and is the standard for pricing credit derivatives. The hazard rate encodes the market-implied [[PD]].
cards.note: Structural models explain why default happens; reduced-form models fit when it is priced to happen.

cards.definition: The quantile of a credit portfolio's loss distribution over a horizon (e.g. one year at 99.9%), capturing default and migration losses. Its distribution is sharply skewed and fat-tailed, so mean and tail diverge far more than in market risk. Inputs include [[PD]], [[LGD]] and correlation; the [[IRB]] formula targets 99.9% Credit VaR.

The tendency of obligors to default together, driven by shared exposure to the economy. It is the single most important — and hardest to estimate — driver of credit portfolio tail risk; small changes move the tail enormously. Enters models via joint [[PD]] distributions and [[LGD]] co-movement.
cards.note: Underestimating it was central to the 2008 structured-credit losses.

cards.definition: Links each obligor's default to a latent variable split into a common factor and idiosyncratic noise, inducing correlated defaults through the shared factor. Tractable and industry-standard, but its thin tails understate joint extreme defaults. The [[ASRF]] model is its portfolio-level closed-form limit for a single common [[PD]].

Assumes one systematic factor and an infinitely granular portfolio, yielding a closed-form tail loss that is portfolio-invariant — the theoretical basis of the Basel [[IRB]] capital formula. Conditional on a bad factor draw, [[PD]] rises sharply.
Extra portfolio risk from large exposures to one obligor or correlated group, which the granular ASRF model assumes away. It is captured by a concentration add-on and large-exposure limits.
cards.note: The IRB formula ignores it by construction, so Pillar 2 must add it back.

cards.definition: The accounting provisioning model requiring banks to book forward-looking expected losses on loans, not just incurred ones. It is probability-weighted across macro scenarios and discounted to present value. Each scenario [[PD]] is combined with [[LGD]] and [[EAD]] to produce a scenario loss.

Loans sit in Stage 1 (performing, 12-month [[ECL]]), Stage 2 (significant increase in credit risk, lifetime [[ECL]]), or Stage 3 (credit-impaired, lifetime [[ECL]] with interest on net balance). The Stage 1→2 trigger is judgmental and provision-defining.
cards.note: Lifetime vs 12-month [[ECL]] is a step change in provisions at the SICR boundary.

cards.term: A loan is classed as non-performing once it is typically {{90}}+ days past due or unlikely to be repaid in full.
cards.definition: A loan in or near default, typically 90+ days past due or unlikely to be repaid in full. The NPL ratio is a headline indicator of asset quality and a focus of supervisory pressure to provision and resolve. Provisioning draws on [[PD]] and [[LGD]] estimates.

cards.term: Loan loss provisions are the capital buffer that absorbs unexpected credit losses.
cards.definition: False. Provisions are a reserve set aside against [[EL|expected credit losses]], reducing reported profit and equity in anticipation of defaults; unexpected loss is absorbed by capital. The coverage ratio (provisions/NPLs) shows how well expected losses are pre-funded.
cards.note: Provisions absorb expected loss; capital absorbs unexpected loss — different layers.
cards.summary: False — provisions absorb expected loss; it is capital that absorbs unexpected loss.

cards.definition: Pooling loans and issuing tradable securities backed by their cash flows, transferring credit risk to investors and freeing balance-sheet capacity. It distributes risk but can obscure where it ends up, as 2008 showed. A [[CDO]] is a securitisation of debt tranches; [[CRM]] techniques can reduce the exposures being securitised.
cards.note: True risk transfer requires the originator not to retain or re-absorb the risk.

cards.definition: Slicing a securitisation's losses into a priority waterfall: junior (equity) tranches absorb first losses, mezzanine next, senior last. Subordination is the credit enhancement that makes senior tranches highly rated despite a risky pool.
cards.formula: loss to a tranche = $\max(0,\,\min(L, U) - A)$ over attachment $A$, detachment $U$

cards.definition: A securitisation of a pool of debt (bonds, loans or other CDOs) sold as rated tranches. Tranche risk is exquisitely sensitive to default correlation — correlation, not just average [[PD]], determines who loses; and [[LGD]] assumptions on the underlying pool drive tranche severity.
cards.note: CDO-squared layered tranching multiplied correlation sensitivity and opacity catastrophically.

cards.definition: Mechanisms that improve a structure's credit quality — subordination, overcollateralisation, excess spread, reserve accounts or third-party guarantees. They protect senior investors by giving losses something to eat through first, in effect reducing [[LGD]] for senior noteholders and [[EAD]] at the senior tranche level.

The risk that a government defaults on or restructures its debt, or imposes transfer/convertibility restrictions. It is a floor and a contagion source for all domestic credit, and the 'risk-free' assumption applied to sovereigns is itself a risk. [[PD]] for sovereigns is observable through [[CDS]] spreads.
cards.note: Sovereign-bank 'doom loop': banks hold sovereign debt, sovereigns backstop banks.

cards.term: Payment-versus-payment systems such as CLS largely neutralise FX settlement (Herstatt) risk by making the two legs of a trade settle simultaneously.
cards.definition: True. Settlement risk is the risk that one side of a trade delivers but the counterparty fails before delivering its leg — acute in FX across time zones. Payment-versus-payment systems (CLS) largely neutralise it by making the legs simultaneous. Named for Bankhaus Herstatt's 1974 failure mid-settlement.
cards.note: Named for Bankhaus Herstatt's 1974 failure mid-settlement.
cards.summary: True — PvP settlement makes both legs conditional on each other, removing the gap where one party pays and the other fails.

cards.definition: Techniques that reduce credit exposure — eligible collateral, guarantees, credit derivatives and netting — recognised in capital rules through substitution or haircut approaches. Effective CRM lowers [[LGD]] or [[EAD]], and thus capital.
cards.note: Recognition requires legal certainty and, for collateral, valuation haircuts.
